coredata.dc:title: Low-cost preparation of temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant amphiphilic carbon dots from a nonionic surfactant and its application in enhanced oil recovery
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Renling
He, Siyu
Wang, Tengfei
Lai, Lu
Zhao, Mingwei
coredata.dc:description: Amphiphilic carbon dots (ACDs) were synthesized from nonionic surfactant alkyl glycosides with different alkyl chains by a low-cost method. Moreover, the surface activity of these ACDs (C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs) was investigated. These ACDs have similar surface tension curves as surfactants, and the surface activity of these ACDs also increases with the alkyl chain length of raw materials. Among these ACDs, C1214-ACDs have the lowest critical micelle concentration (CMC) of 40.00 mg/L, a fifth of the raw material (APG1214). The surface tension of C1214-ACDs is not easily affected by the change in pH. In addition, C1214-ACDs have excellent temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant properties. The interfacial tension of crude oil-water is reduced from 26.59 mN/m to 0.68 mN/m by C1214-ACDs. Furthermore, C1214-ACDs maintains high interfacial activity at c NaCl = 200 g/L and c CaCl2 = 50 g/L, reducing interfacial tension to 0.054 mN/m and 0.048 mN/m. C1214-ACDs have high oil removal efficiency. After treatment with APG1214, C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs, the area of the oil film is reduced by 51.0%, 46.6%, 56.2%, and 78.1%, respectively. Moreover, APG1214 and C1214-ACDs reduce the contact angle of water droplets from 107.4° to 60.5° and 45.6°, respectively, indicating the wettability alternation of C1214-ACDs is better than raw material APG1214. Finally, the oil displacement efficiency of C1214-ACDs is 16.6% in the presence of 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2. Therefore, as temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant nanoparticles with an ultra-small size, ACDs have broad application prospects in enhancing oil recovery in high-temperature and high-salt reservoirs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic carbon dots
Temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant properties
Interfacial properties
Wettability alteration
Enhanced oil recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of oil film removal properties
Effects of inorganic salts on surface activity of ACDs
Effects of pH on surface activity of ACDs
Effects of temperature on surface activity of ACDs
Crude oil-water interfacial properties of ACDs
Effects of inorganic salts on interfacial tension of ACDs
Effects of temperature on interfacial tension of ACDs
The oil film removal and the wettability alteration of ACDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic route of C1214-ACDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

1-syringe pump; 2- six-way valve; 3- crude oil; 4-brine; 5- ACDs solution; 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11 and 13- control valves; 12-core gripper; 14- measuring cylinder. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) FT-IR spectra, and (d) UV–vis spectra of C1214-ACDs. The inset of (a) is the crystalline lattice of the C1214-ACDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XPS spectra of C1214-ACDs; (b) XRD of C1214-ACDs; (c) Photographs of APG1214 and C1214-ACDs in different solvents under daylight (left) and UV light (right) at 1000 mg/L. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Surface tension curves of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs and APG1214 at 25 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effects of NaCl (a) and CaCl2 (b) on the surface tension of C1214-ACDs at different concentrations (10 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 40 mg/L, 300 mg/L, and 1000 mg/L). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Effects of pH on the surface tension of C1214-ACDs solution at different concentrations; (b) Effects of pH on the surface tension of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs, and APG1214 at 300 mg/L. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Effects of temperature on the surface tension of C1214-ACDs aqueous solution at different concentrations; (b) surface tension of C1214-ACDs aqueous solution aged at different temperatures for 12 h without inorganic salt; (c) surface tension of C1214-ACDs aqueous solution aged at different temperatures for 12 h with 8.5 × 104 mg/L NaCl and 1 × 104 mg/L CaCl2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Interfacial tension curves of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs at 60 °C and 6000 r/min; (b) interfacial tension curves of C1214-ACDs and APG1214 at 60 °C and 6000 r/min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effects of NaCl (a) and CaCl2 (b) on the interfacial tension (IFT) of C1214-ACDs at 60 °C; (c) Dynamic interfacial tension curves between the crude oil and water, C1214-ACDs, C1214-ACDs with 3 × 104 mg/L NaCl and 1 × 104 mg/L CaCl2 at 60 °C (the C1214-ACDs concentration is 100 mg/L). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Effects of aging temperature on the IFT of C1214-ACDs solution at different concentrations (a) without inorganic salt and (b) with 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2 at 60 °C; (c) Photographs of C1214-ACDs (100 mg/L) solution with 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2 before and after different aging temperatures for 12 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photographs of oil film removal by C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs, C1214-ACDs with 3 × 104 mg/L NaCl and 1 × 104 mg/L CaCl2 and APG1214 at different treatment times; (b) Relative area of residual oil film at different treatment time after treatment by C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs; (c) Relative area of residual oil film at different treatment time after treatment by APG1214, C1214-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs with 3 × 104 mg/L NaCl and 1 × 104 mg/L CaCl2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Contact angles (θ) of water droplets on the oil-wet glass sheets before and after treatment with C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs solutions, respectively; (b) Contact angles (θ) of water droplets on the oil-wet glass sheets before and after treatment with APG1214, C1214-ACDs, C1214-ACDs with inorganic salts for 48 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Core flooding with APG1214 and C1214-ACDs: (a) injection pressure; (b) oil recovery. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Surface activity parameters of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs, and APG1214.
Comparison of interfacial activity parameters of CDs between this paper and literature data.
Comparison of oil recovery of CDs between this paper and literature data.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: θ (°): Contact angles of water droplets.
C (wt%): Concentration corresponding to the lowest interfacial tension; wt%:weight concentration.
CDs: carbon dots b-CDs: blue-emissive CDs.
hh-CDs: high-hydrophilic CDs CQDs: carbon quantum dots.
S-CQDs: sulfur functionalized CQDs g-CQDs: glucose CQDs.
am-CDs: amino-modified CDs af-CDs: amino-free CDs.
Si-GQDs: silica–graphene quantum dots g-CDs: green emissive CDs.
BS-CDs: 4-amino-benzenesulfonic acid monosodium salt CDs y-CDs: yellow emissive CDs.

head.ce:title: Low-cost preparation of temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant amphiphilic carbon dots from a nonionic surfactant and its application in enhanced oil recovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou, 434023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Unconventional Oil & Gas Development, Ministry of Education, School of Petroleum Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Unconventional Oil & Gas Development

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Unconventional Oil & Gas Development, Ministry of Education, School of Petroleum Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amphiphilic carbon dots (ACDs) were synthesized from nonionic surfactant alkyl glycosides with different alkyl chains by a low-cost method. Moreover, the surface activity of these ACDs (C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs) was investigated. These ACDs have similar surface tension curves as surfactants, and the surface activity of these ACDs also increases with the alkyl chain length of raw materials. Among these ACDs, C1214-ACDs have the lowest critical micelle concentration (CMC) of 40.00 mg/L, a fifth of the raw material (APG1214). The surface tension of C1214-ACDs is not easily affected by the change in pH. In addition, C1214-ACDs have excellent temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant properties. The interfacial tension of crude oil-water is reduced from 26.59 mN/m to 0.68 mN/m by C1214-ACDs. Furthermore, C1214-ACDs maintains high interfacial activity at c NaCl = 200 g/L and c CaCl2 = 50 g/L, reducing interfacial tension to 0.054 mN/m and 0.048 mN/m.

C1214-ACDs have high oil removal efficiency. After treatment with APG1214, C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs, the area of the oil film is reduced by 51.0%, 46.6%, 56.2%, and 78.1%, respectively. Moreover, APG1214 and C1214-ACDs reduce the contact angle of water droplets from 107.4° to 60.5° and 45.6°, respectively, indicating the wettability alternation of C1214-ACDs is better than raw material APG1214. Finally, the oil displacement efficiency of C1214-ACDs is 16.6% in the presence of 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2. Therefore, as temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant nanoparticles with an ultra-small size, ACDs have broad application prospects in enhancing oil recovery in high-temperature and high-salt reservoirs.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are ultrafine materials with at least one dimension on the nanometer scale and have attracted more attention in oil and gas development due to their unique surface and small size effect. The mechanism of nanomaterials application in enhanced oil recovery mainly includes reducing oil-water interfacial tension [1], generating separation pressure [2], changing the wettability of reservoir rock [3], and reducing the fluidity ratio [4]. Compared to surfactants, nanoparticles may increase surface roughness and enhance wettability performance by adsorbing on the surface of the rock core to form a specific micro-nano structure [5,6]; nanomaterials are less affected by the formation temperature and mineralization [7].

So far, the reported oil-displacement nanomaterials mainly include metal oxide nanomaterials [8–11], nano-SiO2 [12], carbon nanomaterials [13], and amphiphilic Janus nanoparticles [14]. However, most oil-displacement nanomaterials have large particle sizes and low interfacial activity without amphiphilicity. Surface modifications are often used to obtain amphiphilic properties, inevitably leading to increased costs and stability issues. Therefore, the low-cost preparation of oil-displacement nanomaterials with an ultra-small size and high interfacial activity remains a technical bottleneck for applying nanotechnology in enhancing oil recovery in ultra-low permeability reservoirs.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with a 1 nm–10 nm size and the ability to emit fluorescence. CDs have enormous potential applications in oil fields, such as reducing pressure and increasing injection, enhancing oil recovery, and improving imbibition performance. For example, Zhao et al. prepared hydrophilic carbon dots using the electrochemical method and mixed them with Tween-80 to prepare carbon nanofluids, which could enhance oil recovery by 24% [15]. Ranjbar et al. synthesized green and yellow fluorescent carbon dots by solvothermal method using phenylenediamine and tartaric acid as raw materials [16]. These carbon dots were used to prepare nanofluids for tracing and enhanced oil recovery in oil fields. Wu et al. prepared high-hydrophilic CDs using petroleum coke as a carbon source, and it could achieve a pressure-reducing rate of 23.81% and enhance oil recovery by 26.38% without any surfactants [17]. Some studies have shown that CDs have a promising application in enhanced recovery [18–20]. However, the CDs reported above usually have strong hydrophilicity but not amphiphilicity, and the surface activity is not high. The matrix wall of low-permeability reservoirs is weakly hydrophilic, and the adsorption of hydrophilic CDs on the wall is weak; hydrophobic CDs are difficult to disperse in the displacement fluid effectively.

Amphiphilic CDs (ACDs) can be adsorbed like surfactant molecules on the matrix wall to achieve a wettability alternation. In addition, ACDs can significantly reduce surface tension and oil-water interfacial tension. These effects are beneficial to enhance oil recovery. ACDs have been used in cell membrane fluorescence imaging [21,22], bacterial detection [23], ion detection [24], and drug carrying [25]. So far, the relevant research mainly focused on the optical properties of ACDs in different solvents [26], the biomarkers [27], and the transmembrane properties of ACDs [28]. However, there are few studies on the surface and interfacial activities and application of ACDs in the oil field. In this paper, ACDs were prepared in one step at a low cost using alkyl glycosides as raw materials. Compared with existing literature, the purification steps of CDs are very simple. Because the synthetic feedstock is a surfactant, it can enhance oil recovery without purification. These CDs have excellent temperature and salt resistance properties, so they have broad application prospects in enhanced oil recovery in high-temperature and high-salt reservoirs.

Alkyl glucosides (APG06, APG10, APG1214, 50%) were purchased from Yuan Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0%–38.0%) and xylene (>99%) are provided by Chengdu Kolon Chemical Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). Crude oil was obtained from China National Offshore Oil Corporation (CNOOC) oil field, and the contents of saturated hydrocarbons, aromatic hydrocarbons, waxes, resins, and asphaltenes were 50.54%, 19.57%, 5.73%, 18.55%, and 4.17%, respectively.

The 6700 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA) was used to obtain the infrared spectra of ACDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was used to characterize the morphology and size distribution of as-prepared ACDs by a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. The UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out by a TU1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument, China). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was used to analyze the surface composition and chemical valence state of C1214-ACDs. The crystal structure type of C1214-ACDs could be examined by a powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) equipped with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 × 10−10 m, 40 kV, 30 mA) radiation at a scanning speed of 3°/min in the range from 5 to 60°.

Fig. 1 shows that ACDs were synthesized using a straightforward synthetic route. First, alkyl glycosides (APG1214, 12.0 g) were added into the flask and heated at 180 °C for 9 h in an oil bath. Next, the obtained products were dissolved in xylene and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the traces of undissolved material because APG1214 could not dissolve in xylene. Finally, after removing the solvent by rotary evaporation, ACDs were obtained by drying in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h, affording a yellow powder product, and the product yield was 84.35%. The as-prepared ACDs were named as C1214-ACDs. According to the same procedure, C6-ACDs and C10-ACDs were synthesized from APG06 and APG10, respectively.

The surface tension of the ACDs solution was measured through the Wilhelmy plate method using a K11 automatic surface tensiometer (Krüss, Germany) at 25.0 °C. Each measurement was repeated three times to ensure the reproducibility of the data.

The interfacial tension between ACDs solutions and crude oil was measured by a TX-500C spinning drop interfacial tensiometer (Zhongchen, Shanghai, China) at 60 °C and 6000 rpm. Each measurement was repeated three times to ensure the reproducibility of the data.

The glass sheets were washed successively 2–3 times with distilled water, acetone, and absolute ethanol and then dried in the oven at 50 °C for 1 h. Then, the glass sheets were immersed in HCl solution (1.0 wt%) for 4 h. Next, the glass sheets were rinsed with distilled water and dried in the oven at 50 °C for 1 h. Finally, the dried glass sheets were immersed in an ethanol solution of dimethyl silicone oil (5.0 v/v%) for seven days for lipophilic treatment [29].

The lipophilic-treated glass sheets were immersed in the ACDs solutions for 48 h and then placed at room temperature for 3 h to dry naturally. The contact angles of water droplets in the air were measured using a DSA30 contact angle meter (Krüss, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of oil film removal properties
ce:section.ce:para: The lipophilic treatment of glass sheets was employed according to the method depicted in section 2.5, and then the glass sheets were evenly coated with crude oil and aged at 60 °C for 48 h. Next, the glass sheets coated with crude oil were immersed in ACDs solutions to observe the oil film removal properties at different times, and the relative area of oil film removal was calculated using Image J graphical analysis software (National Institutes of Health, USA). The analysis software was used to convert the image into a grayscale image, and the grayscale value of the area can be obtained by selecting the oil film area in the grayscale image. The degree of oil film removal is expressed as the relative area of the oil film before and after immersing.

The schematic diagram of the flooding equipment is shown in Fig. 2 . The experimental temperature was set to 60 °C, and the parameters of the sandstone core applied in this experiment are shown in Table 1 . The experimental steps are as follows:
1)
The aqueous phase permeability of the cores was measured. The core was placed in the core gripper, and water (5 pore volume (PV)) was injected into the core. The permeability of the core was estimated by Darcy's law.
2)
Ten PV of crude oil was injected into the core until the oil content in the expelled liquid reached 98 % to determine the saturated oil content of the core.
3)
The saturated cores were placed in a drying oven and aged at 120 °C for 12 h.
4)
Two PV of brine with a salinity of 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2 was injected into the core to measure waterflood efficiency.
5)
One-half PV of APG1214 and C1214-ACDs solution was injected into the core to measure crude oil recovery.
6)
One PV of brine was injected into the core to measure crude oil recovery after the adsorption of APG1214 and C1214-ACDs.

TEM was used to characterize the morphology and size distribution of C1214-ACDs. Fig. 3 (a) shows that C1214-ACDs exhibit a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation. The size distribution of C1214-ACDs is between 1.5 nm and 5.3 nm, and the average size of C1214-ACDs is 3.3 nm in Fig. 3(b). The inset of Fig. 3(a) shows that C1214-ACDs exhibit a distinct lattice structure of 0.22 nm, which is similar to that of the single-crystal graphitic carbon lattice structure, suggesting that there is a highly crystalline graphitic structure in the C1214-ACDs [30]. In Fig. 3(c), the infrared spectrum of C1214-ACDs is similar to that of APG1214, and the O–H stretching vibration peaks (3600 cm−1∼3000 cm−1) and C–H stretching vibration peaks (3000 cm−1∼2800 cm−1) of alkyl chains can be observed [31]. C1214-ACDs exhibit characteristic adsorption peaks at 775 and 1400 cm−1, corresponding to the C–H stretching of an alkyl chain, and at 1030 cm−1, corresponding to the symmetric stretching vibrations of the C–O group. In addition, a peak is observed at 1640 cm−1, corresponding to the C–O–C adsorption peaks of glucopyranose [32,33]. The results indicate that the surface of C1214-ACDs has both hydrophilic hydroxyl groups and hydrophobic alkyl chains. However, compared with APG1214, the absorption peak intensity of C1214-ACDs related to hydroxyl, alkyl chain, and other related functional groups becomes small, which indicates that the number of these functional groups decreases during the carbonization of APG1214. Fig. 3(d) is the UV absorption spectra of C1214-ACDs aqueous solution. The absorption peak in 223.0 nm is attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of the CC skeleton of C1214-ACDs [34]. Compared with the APG1214, C1214-ACDs exhibit a strong absorbance at 223.0 nm, confirming an increasing conjugate structure. On the other hand, a weak absorption peak at 268.0 nm is attributed to the n-π* electronic transition of the functional groups located on the C1214-ACDs surface.

XPS was used to analyze the surface composition and chemical valence state of C1214-ACDs. In Fig. 4 (a), it can be seen from C1s high-resolution spectrum that the surface of C1214-ACDs exhibits CC/C–C (284.7 eV), C–O (286.5 eV), and CO (287.7 eV) [35], indicating that the surface of C1214-ACDs contains wealthy functional groups such as –OH and –COOH, which is in good agreement with FT-IR result. As shown in Fig. 4(b), it is confirmed by XRD patterns that C1214-ACDs have a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 20°, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic diffraction of the standard graphite structure, indicating that the carbon core of the C1214-ACDs has an amorphous graphite structure [36].

The daylight and UV photographs of APG1214 and C1214-ACDs dissolved in water and different polar solvents are shown in Fig. 4(c). APG1214 is completely dissolved in water, slightly dissolved in chloroform, and hardly dissolved in ethanol and xylene. However, C1214-ACDs are slightly dissolved in ethanol and can be completely dispersed in water, chloroform, and xylene. In addition, C1214-ACDs have strong fluorescence in both water and organic solvents, further confirming their amphiphilicity.

Fig. 5 shows the equilibrium surface tension curves of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs, and APG1214 at 25 °C. As the ACDs concentration increases, the surface tension of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs aqueous solution gradually decreases and remains constant. Notably, the surface tension curves of these ACDs are similar to those of conventional surfactants. According to the breakpoint of the surface tension curves, the critical micelle concentrations (CMC) of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs are 4000.00 mg/L, 1500.00 mg/L, and 40.00 mg/L, respectively, while their γ CMC is 30.58 mN/m, 30.14 mN/m, and 27.18 mN/m, respectively. Carbonization and polymerization of raw materials can enhance their hydrophobicity during the preparation of ACDs [37]. Therefore, ACDs are easier to diffuse to the gas-liquid interface, which improves the surface activity of ACDs. Like conventional surfactants, the increase in the alkyl chain length decreases the CMC and γ CMC of ACDs. The corresponding surface property parameters are listed in Table 2 . As shown in Table 2, the CMC of C1214-ACDs is a fifth of raw material APG1214, and the γ CMC of C1214-ACDs is also lower than APG1214, may result from the increased hydrophobicity during carbonization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of inorganic salts on surface activity of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the surface activity of C1214-ACDs at different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), at 10 mg/L and 20 mg/L, the surface tension of the C1214-ACDs solution decreases first and then increases slightly. When C1214-ACDs concentration is equal to or greater than 40 mg/L, the addition of NaCl has little effect on the surface tension of C1214-ACDs. When the NaCl concentration reaches saturation (360 g/L at 25 °C), the surface tension of C1214-ACDs is still 28.54 mN/m at 40 mg/L. In Fig. 6 (b), the effects of CaCl2 on the surface tension of C1214-ACDs solution are slightly higher than that of NaCl, but the surface tension of C1214-ACDs at 40 mg/L is 33.13 mN/m in a saturated CaCl2 solution (750 g/L at 25 °C). The results of FT-IR and XPS indicate that there are more hydroxyl groups on the surface of C1214-ACDs, which have strong hydrogen bonding and weak hydration ability. Therefore, the inorganic salts have less influence on the surface activity of C1214-ACDs. These results indicate that the C1214-ACDs can be tolerant to saturated salt water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on surface activity of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of pH on the surface tension of C1214-ACDs solution at different concentrations are shown in Fig. 7 (a). The surface tension of the C1214-ACDs solution is almost unaffected by changing pH values from 1 to 11, indicating that C1214-ACDs are stable in strong acid and basic environments. Fig. 7(b) reflects the effects of pH on the surface tension of ACDs and APG1214 at 300 mg/L. It can be seen that C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs, and APG1214 are not sensitive to pH. APG has excellent acid and basic resistance performance, and the hydrophilic group of ACDs originated from APG. Therefore, ACDs are stable in acidic and basic environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature on surface activity of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the surface tension of the C1214-ACDs solution decreases with the increase in temperature. As the concentration increases, the effects of temperature on the surface tension become weak. The increasing temperature decreases the hydrogen bond interaction between the hydrophilic groups on the surface of C1214-ACDs and water molecules, which enhances the hydrophobicity of C1214-ACDs and reduces the surface tension of the C1214-ACDs aqueous solution [38]. Moreover, the C1214-ACDs solution was aged at a specific temperature for 12 h to evaluate its temperature-resistance properties further. Fig. 8(b) reflects the effects of aging temperature on the surface activity of the C1214-ACDs solution without inorganic salt. The surface tension of the C1214-ACDs solution almost remains constant with increasing temperature. For example, at 40 mg/L and 100 mg/L, the surface tension of C1214-ACDs concentration increases merely by about 1 mN/m as the aging temperature rises to 140 °C. Fig. 8(c) reflects the effects of aging temperature on the surface activity of C1214-ACDs solution with 2 × 105 mg/L salinity (8.5 × 104 mg/L NaCl and 1 × 104 mg/L CaCl2). The results show that the surface tension of the C1214-ACDs solution decreases slightly and then remains constant. At 10 mg/L, the surface tension of the C1214-ACDs solution decreases from 40.01 mN/m to 37.83 mN/m as the aging temperature increases from 25 °C to 140 °C. C1214-ACDs could form high-temperature-resistant graphene-like structures during polycondensation and carbonization, thus C1214-ACDs have high-temperature-resistant properties. The above results indicate that C1214-ACDs are excellent temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant, thus suitable for high-temperature and high-salinity reservoirs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crude oil-water interfacial properties of ACDs

ce:section.ce:para: The interfacial tension curves of ACDs aqueous solution with crude oil at 60 °C are shown in Fig. 9 . Fig. 9(a) shows the equilibrium interfacial tension curves of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs at 60 °C. As the ACDs concentration increases, the interfacial tension sharply decreases and remains constant. According to the breakpoint of the interfacial tension curves, the CMC of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs are 5000.00 mg/L, 2000.00 mg/L, and 100.00 mg/L, respectively. The equilibrium interfacial tension of C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs and C1214-ACDs are 2.48 mN/m, 0.84 mN/m, and 0.68 mN/m, respectively. ACDs have hydrophilic groups and hydrophobic alkyl chains, which promote their diffusion towards the oil-water interface, thereby reducing the oil-water interfacial tension. Furthermore, the increase of hydrophobic alkyl chains in ACDs significantly reduces oil-water interfacial tension. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the C1214-ACD solution has lower CMC than APG1214, which may result from the increased hydrophobicity of C1214-ACDs after carbonization. The interfacial tension of ACDs synthesized in this paper and CDs reported by some references are provided in Table 3 , indicating that the as-prepared C1214-ACDs have the lowest CMC and interfacial tension [39–42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of inorganic salts on interfacial tension of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows that the interfacial tension between crude oil and water is 26.59 mN/m, and adding NaCl or CaCl2 decreases the interfacial tension gradually. However, for the C1214-ACDs solution, the interfacial tension first decreases and then increases with NaCl concentration in Fig. 10(a). At c NaCl = 200 g/L, the interfacial tension reaches the lowest value at different C1214-ACDs concentrations. Meanwhile, when the C1214-ACDs concentration is 100 mg/L, the interfacial tension of the C1214-ACDs solution is lower than 0.1 mN/m at the NaCl concentration between 50 g/L and 300 g/L, and the lowest interfacial tension is 0.054 mN/m. The interfacial tension of C1214-ACDs in the saturated NaCl solution is lower than in the absence of NaCl, indicating these C1214-ACDs have a high Na+ resistance property. In Fig. 10(b), the addition of CaCl2 significantly affects the interfacial tension of the C1214-ACDs solution. The interfacial tension values of the C1214-ACDs solution at different concentrations first decrease and then increase with CaCl2. At the CaCl2 concentration of 50 g/L, the lowest IFT occurs, which is 0.048 mN/m. Inorganic salts could interact with substances in crude oil to produce interfacial active substances, which have a synergistic effect with C1214-ACDs, thereby significantly reducing the oil-water interfacial tension. In addition, the salting-out effect decreases the CMC of C1214-ACDs and reduces the interfacial oil-water tension [43]. However, when the concentration of inorganic salts is too high, the salting-out effect makes C1214-ACDs easy to associate with and form large micelles, which reduces the interfacial activity.

Fig. 10(c) shows the dynamic interfacial tension curves between crude oil and C1214-ACDs solutions. The dynamic interfacial tension curves reflect the change of interfacial tension with time. Due to the diffusion and adsorption of C1214-ACDs at the oil-water interface, the interfacial tension decreases gradually until equilibrium. Fig. 10(c) shows that C1214-ACDs with inorganic salts exhibit a rapid interfacial adsorption rate and low interfacial tension. As a result, C1214-ACDs have great potential applications for enhanced oil recovery in high-salinity reservoirs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature on interfacial tension of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of aging temperature on the interfacial activity of C1214-ACDs aqueous solution without inorganic salts and in the presence of 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2 are shown in Fig. 11 (a) and (b), respectively. At 40 mg/L, the interfacial tension of the C1214-ACDs aqueous solution remains constant. At 100 mg/L and 500 mg/L, the interfacial tension of the C1214-ACDs aqueous solution after aging for 12 h is slightly reduced. For example, when the C1214-ACDs concentration is 100 mg/L, the IFT of C1214-ACDs solution decreases from 1.73 mN/m to 0.89 mN/m as the aging temperature increases from 25 °C to 140 °C; however, the IFT of C1214-ACDs solution in the presence of 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2 decreases to 0.18 mN/m as the aging temperature increases to 140 °C. Fig. 11(c) shows that the C1214-ACDs solution remains transparent after aging, and there is no significant aggregation of C1214-ACDs solutions at high temperatures. The above results show that the C1214-ACDs have excellent temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The oil film removal and the wettability alteration of ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: During water flooding, the wettability of reservoir rock will affect the relative oil/water permeability, capillary pressure, and water drive efficiency. The change in wettability significantly impacts the efficiency of oil removal. Fig. 12 (a) shows the crude oil film treated by C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs, and APG1214 at different times. Fig. 12(b) and (c) show the variation of the relative area of oil film with time. After treatment with C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs for 24 h, the area of the oil film is reduced by 46.6%, 56.2%, and 78.1%, respectively. The area of the oil film decreases significantly in the C1214-ACDs solution, while half of the oil film is still not effectively removed in the C6-ACDs and C10-ACDs solution. Furthermore, the oil film removal efficiency of C1214-ACDs is greater than that of raw material APG1214, while the relative area of the oil film treated by APG1214 solutions is reduced by only 51.0%. With the addition of inorganic salts, the area of the oil film is reduced by 81.5%, indicating that inorganic salts have a synergistic effect with C1214-ACDs in the oil film removal.

Fig. 13 shows the contact angle of water droplets on the surface of oil-wet glass plates before and after treatment by C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, C1214-ACDs, and APG1214. Because C6-ACDs are more hydrophilic, C6-ACDs reduce the contact angle of water droplets from 107.9° to 74.8°, making the oil film easily adhere to the oil-wet glass sheets. Meanwhile, C10-ACDs can reduce the water contact angle from 108.3° to 64.5°. The wettability alteration of C10-ACDs is significantly greater than C6-ACDs, promoting the complete removal of the oil film from oil-wet glass sheets; thus, the residual area of the oil film for C10-ACDs is lower than C6-ACDs. With the increased alkyl chain of ACDs, the contact angle is more inclined to water-wet, which improves the oil film removal efficiency. Moreover, APG1214 reduces the contact angle of water droplets from 108.5° to 60.5°, while C1214-ACDs can reduce the water contact angle from 107.4° to 45.6°. The wettability alteration of C1214-ACDs is significantly greater than that of APG1214, thus promoting the separation of crude oil from the rock surface.

Core flooding experiments are employed to evaluate the potential of C1214-ACDs for enhanced oil recovery. Fig. 14 (a) reflects the evolution of injection pressure during core floods using APG1214 and C1214-ACDs as the agent of tertiary flooding. It can be seen from Fig. 14(a) that the injection pressure shows a measurable decline after the injection of APG1214 and C1214-ACDs. Moreover, the injection pressure of C1214-ACDs is lower than that of APG1214. This result can be explained by their ability to alter the wettability of the oil-wet rock surface towards more water-wet. As shown in Fig. 13, the wettability alteration of C1214-ACDs is more significant than APG1214, which reduces water flow resistance in the porous media. In addition, the injection pressure of the subsequent brine flooding exhibits a continuous decrease after the injection of APG1214 and C1214-ACDs because of wettability alteration caused by the adsorption of APG1214 and C1214-ACDs on the rock surface.

Oil recovery curves for APG1214 and C1214-ACDs flooding are shown in Fig. 14(b). As observed, in both cases, the oil recovered with brine flooding is approximately 60%, and the oil recovery exhibits a substantial increase after the injection of APG1214 or C1214-ACDs. This step injects 0.5 PV of APG1214 or C1214-ACDs solution according to the actual case. However, the oil displacement efficiency of C1214-ACDs is significantly higher than that of APG1214. The crude oil recovery increased by 8.8% and 16.6% after APG1214 and C1214-ACDs flooding, respectively. This can be attributed to the ability of C1214-ACDs to alter wettability and reduce oil-water interfacial tension, which makes oil droplets more easily deformed and removed from the rock surface. The oil recovery of ACDs synthesized in this paper and CDs reported by some references are provided in Table 4 [16,17,41,44–47], indicating that the as-prepared C1214-ACDs have suitable temperature and salt resistance. This research provides a feasible approach for developing and applying ACDs with good thermal stability and salt resistance in unconventional reservoir development.

C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs were synthesized cheaply from nonionic surfactant alkyl glycosides. C1214-ACDs have the best surface activity among these ACDs. The CMC of C1214-ACDs is 40.00 mg/L, and the γ CMC is 27.18 mN/m. Due to the raw material being a nonionic surfactant, C1214-ACDs have high stability and are not easily affected by acids and alkalis. In addition, C1214-ACDs have excellent temperature-resistant and salt-tolerant properties. The surface tension of the C1214-ACDs solution slightly decreases as the aging temperature increases from 25 °C to 140 °C in the presence of 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2.

Furthermore, C1214-ACDs can maintain high interfacial activity at c NaCl = 200 g/L and c CaCl2 = 50 g/L, reducing interfacial tension to 0.054 mN/m and 0.048 mN/m. C1214-ACDs have high oil removal efficiency and excellent wettability alternation. After treatment with APG1214, C6-ACDs, C10-ACDs, and C1214-ACDs for 24 h, the area of the oil film is reduced by 51.0%, 46.6%, 56.2%, and 78.1%. The increase in alkyl chain length is conducive to the adsorption of ACDs on the surface of oil-wet glass plates. Moreover, APG1214 reduces the contact angle of water droplets from 108.5° to 60.5°, while C1214-ACDs can reduce the contact angle from 107.4° to 45.6°. Thus, the wettability alternation of C1214-ACDs is better than APG1214. Core flooding experiments show that the oil displacement efficiency of C1214-ACDs is significantly higher than APG1214, and the oil displacement efficiency of C1214-ACDs is 16.6% in the presence of 85 g/L NaCl and 10 g/L CaCl2. This research provides a feasible approach for developing and applying ACDs with good thermal stability and salt resistance in unconventional reservoir development.

Renling Lu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Siyu He: Investigation. Tengfei Wang: Supervision, Data curation. Lu Lai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mingwei Zhao: Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars (52222403), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074333), and Taishan Scholar Young Expert (tsqn202211079).
